title: How to Compost in Winter Without Problems
excerpt: Winter composting usually moves more slowly, but it does not have to stop. With a larger pile, enough dry browns, protection from excess moisture, and realistic expectations, you can keep composting through cold weather without major trouble.
author: Permatable Team
content:
Winter Composting

Composting in winter can seem difficult at first. Cold temperatures slow decomposition, frozen scraps are harder to work with, and the pile may not look very active for weeks at a time. But winter composting is still absolutely possible, and in many cases, it is easier than people expect once the system is set up properly.

The main thing to understand is that winter compost usually moves more slowly than compost in warm weather. That does not mean the process has stopped. With a few simple adjustments, you can keep composting through the colder months without major problems.

Does Composting Stop in Winter?

Not completely. Composting usually slows down in winter, but it does not always stop.

What happens depends on:

  • How cold your climate gets
  • The size of your pile
  • How well the pile holds heat
  • What materials you add
  • Whether the pile freezes fully or only partially

In mild winters, compost may keep breaking down slowly the entire season. In colder areas, the outer parts of the pile may freeze while the center stays somewhat active for a while. Even if the pile becomes mostly dormant, it often restarts when temperatures rise.

Why Winter Composting Still Matters

Many gardeners keep composting in winter because kitchen scraps and yard waste do not stop just because the weather changes.

Winter composting helps you:

  • Continue reducing household waste
  • Avoid storing too many scraps indoors
  • Keep materials building up for spring
  • Make use of fall leaves and seasonal organic waste
  • Stay consistent with your composting routine

Even if breakdown is slow, the pile is still being prepared for faster decomposition later.

Start Winter With a Healthy Pile

The best time to prepare for winter composting is before the cold weather arrives.

A well-prepared compost pile going into winter should:

  • Have a good mix of greens and browns
  • Be large enough to hold some heat
  • Not be too wet
  • Contain enough dry material for balance
  • Already be actively decomposing if possible

A pile that is healthy in autumn usually handles winter much better than one that is poorly balanced from the start.

Make the Pile Large Enough

Small piles lose heat quickly in cold weather. A larger pile has a better chance of staying active longer into winter.

If possible, aim for a compost pile around:

  • 3 feet wide
  • 3 feet long
  • 3 feet high

This size often helps the center retain some warmth. Very small bins can still compost in winter, but they tend to slow down more dramatically.

Use Extra Browns

Winter composting often works best when you have plenty of browns available. Kitchen scraps continue to build up, but dry materials may be harder to find once snow or rain arrives.

Useful browns for winter include:

  • Dry leaves saved from fall
  • Shredded cardboard
  • Newspaper
  • Straw
  • Paper towels
  • Plain paper egg cartons

Keeping a bag or bin of browns nearby makes winter composting much easier. Every time you add kitchen scraps, cover them with dry material to help control moisture and odor.

Save Fall Leaves for Winter

One of the smartest things you can do is store dry leaves in autumn for winter use. Leaves are one of the best compost browns, and they are especially useful when fresh greens from the garden are no longer available.

Stored leaves can help you:

  • Balance wet food scraps
  • Insulate the pile
  • Reduce smell
  • Improve airflow
  • Keep winter composting simple

Even a few bags of saved leaves can make a big difference.

Keep Adding Kitchen Scraps Carefully

You can keep composting kitchen scraps in winter, but it helps to do it thoughtfully.

Good winter compost scraps include:

  • Fruit and vegetable peels
  • Coffee grounds
  • Tea leaves
  • Crushed eggshells

To avoid problems:

  • Bury scraps in the pile if possible
  • Cover them with browns
  • Avoid dumping large wet loads all at once
  • Skip meat, dairy, and oily foods

In freezing weather, scraps may sit longer before breaking down, so balance is especially important.

Chop Scraps Smaller

Large scraps freeze more slowly and break down more slowly. Chopping kitchen waste into smaller pieces makes winter composting easier and helps materials decompose faster once the pile warms again.

This is especially helpful for:

  • Vegetable peels
  • Fruit scraps
  • Stems
  • Cardboard
  • Leaves

Smaller pieces also make it easier to mix the pile when conditions allow.

Protect the Pile From Too Much Moisture

Winter compost problems often come from excess moisture more than cold alone. Rain, sleet, and snow can make a pile soggy, heavy, and poorly aerated.

To reduce this problem:

  • Use a covered bin if possible
  • Place a loose cover over the pile during heavy rain
  • Avoid sealing it too tightly
  • Make sure the base drains reasonably well

The goal is to keep out excess water while still allowing air to move through the pile.

Insulate the Pile Naturally

You do not need fancy materials to help protect a winter compost pile. A little natural insulation can help the pile hold warmth longer.

Good insulating materials include:

  • Straw
  • Dry leaves
  • Cardboard
  • Unfinished compost around the outside
  • Snow, in some cases, as an outer insulating layer

The center of the pile matters most, so even if the outer layer is cold, the inside may stay more stable for longer.

Should You Turn Compost in Winter?

Usually, less turning is better in very cold weather.

Turning a pile releases stored heat, which can cool it down quickly in winter. If the pile is already active and warm, turning may still help in mild conditions. But in freezing weather, it is often better to disturb it less and focus on layering scraps and browns.

A practical approach is:

  • Turn only if the pile is too wet or badly compacted
  • Otherwise leave it mostly alone during the coldest periods

Once temperatures rise, you can resume turning more actively.

What if the Compost Freezes?

A frozen compost pile is not a failure. It simply means decomposition has paused or slowed significantly.

When compost freezes:

  • Keep adding suitable scraps if your system can handle it
  • Continue covering new additions with browns
  • Do not worry if nothing seems to happen for a while

When the weather warms, microbial activity usually starts up again and the pile continues breaking down.

Indoor Scrap Storage Can Help

During very cold weather, some people prefer to collect kitchen scraps indoors for a few days before taking them outside.

Helpful storage options include:

  • A small countertop container
  • A sealed bin under the sink
  • A freezer container for scraps

Freezing scraps indoors can reduce smell and fruit flies while making it easier to manage winter composting at your own pace.

Winter Composting Mistakes to Avoid

A few mistakes can make winter composting harder than it needs to be.

  • Letting the pile get waterlogged: too much moisture is a major problem in cold weather
  • Running out of browns: without dry material, winter food scraps can become messy quickly
  • Turning too often in freezing conditions: this can release heat and slow the pile even more
  • Expecting summer-level activity: winter composting is naturally slower
  • Giving up when the pile looks inactive: cold compost often restarts just fine in spring

What to Expect by Spring

By the end of winter, your compost may not be fully finished, but that does not mean progress was lost. In many cases, the pile will contain a mix of partly broken-down material that becomes active again as temperatures rise.

Once spring arrives, you can:

  • Turn the pile
  • Check the moisture level
  • Add fresh greens and browns
  • Restart more active composting

Winter materials often become the base of a strong spring compost pile.

Final Thoughts

You can compost in winter without problems if you adjust your expectations and prepare the pile properly. Composting will usually slow down in cold weather, but it does not have to stop. A larger pile, plenty of browns, protection from excess moisture, and a little insulation can keep the system working through the season.

Winter composting is less about speed and more about continuity. Keep the habit going, and when warmer weather returns, your compost pile will be ready to pick up where it left off.
